title: NAIS
subtitle: Robots that understand "I'm thirsty" — and figure out where to go
teaser: A ROS 2 framework that lets mobile robots build a live map of meaning — objects, rooms, relations — and navigate from natural requests like "I'm thirsty".
intro: Tell today's robots "go to coordinates 3.2, 5.7" and they will oblige. Tell them "I'm thirsty" and they are lost. NAIS (Navigation AI Impulse by Scene Graph) closes that gap: it is a modular ROS 2 framework that lets a mobile robot build a live map of meaning — which objects are in which rooms, and how they relate — and use it to act on everyday human requests.

why.text: For robots to be useful in homes, offices, and care settings, people should not have to speak 'robot'. The robot should understand people. Turning vague, human phrases into sensible actions — finding a water dispenser when someone is thirsty, a chair when someone is tired — is a key step toward machines that fit into daily life instead of demanding technical skills from their users.

sections.title: A map of meaning, not just geometry
sections.text: While exploring, the robot combines classical SLAM mapping with a dynamic scene graph: a living diagram of objects inside rooms, and rooms inside buildings. Vision-language models describe what the camera sees in open vocabulary — no fixed list of allowed objects — while language models infer the spatial relations between them. The map updates continuously, in real time.

sections.title: From "I'm thirsty" to a navigation plan
sections.text: When a person makes a request, a language model interprets the intent and searches the scene graph for a sensible target — a cup, a water dispenser — then hands the goal to the classical planner that drives the robot there safely. Demonstrated on a TurtleBot 4 in real office environments, NAIS handles context-dependent commands without manual configuration.

sections.title: Modular and open by design
sections.text: NAIS is the first ROS 2 framework to unify SLAM, open-vocabulary scene graphs, and foundation-model reasoning in one deployable pipeline. Each stage — mapping, understanding, reasoning, planning — is a replaceable module, so researchers can swap in new models as AI evolves, instead of rebuilding the system from scratch.
